title: The Disutility of Stock Market Losses: Evidence From Domestic Violence
abstract: Stock returns during the week are negatively associated with the reported incidence of domestic violence during the weekend. This relationship is primarily driven by negative returns. The incidence of domestic violence increases with the magnitude of losses, and the effect increases with local stock market participation. Our findings suggest that negative wealth shocks caused by stock market crashes can affect stress levels within intimate relationships, escalate arguments, and trigger domestic violence. Stock market losses may reduce household utility beyond the shock to financial wealth, supporting gain-loss models where disutility from losses outweighs the utility from gains of a similar magnitude.

author.Tse-Chun Lin: University of Hong Kong, China and National Taiwan University,Taiwan
author.Vesa Pursiainen*: University of St. Gallen and Swiss Finance Institute,Switzerland

article.1. Literature Review and Hypothesis Deve:
JEL D14 - Household Saving; Personal Finance D62 - Externalities G41 - Role and Effects of Psychological, Emotional, Social, and Cognitive Factors on Decision Making in Financial Markets G51 - Household Saving, Borrowing, Debt, and Wealth J12 - Marriage; Marital Dissolution; Family Structure; Domestic Abuse Issue Section: Article Editor: Lauren Cohen Lauren Cohen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Financial losses may entail negative emotional shocks, while gains can cause positive ones. Meanwhile, the prospect theory ofKahneman and Tversky (1979)suggests that the disutility from negative wealth shocks is different from the utility from equivalent gains. This asymmetric utility from stock returns is used as a foundation for a number of models seeking to explain empirical patterns in the financial markets, including the equity premium puzzle (Benartzi and Thaler 1995) and the disposition effect (e.g.,Odean 1998;Barberis 2013). However, the empirical findings for the asymmetric utility of gains and losses come mostly from experimental settings, with limited real-world evidence.1In this paper, we study the asymmetric relationship between stock market gains and losses and the incidence of domestic violence.

We focus on domestic violence as it is one of the most common types of crime as well as a substantial public health problem. Nearly one-third of women and more than a quarter of men in the United States experience physical violence by an intimate partner in their lifetime, while the estimated annual cost of domestic violence against women alone is more than $5.8 billion.2Economic models of household bargaining that incorporate domestic violence typically suggest that violence may arise because it provides positive utility to the perpetrator, while the victims’ outside options determine their willingness to suffer domestic violence.3Card and Dahl (2011)develop a loss-of-control model to analyze how domestic violence can be triggered unintentionally when an argument escalates out of control. They also find compelling evidence of domestic violence being triggered by emotional shocks related to losses in football when the home team is expected to win.

Money is one of the most common sources of stress generally and in intimate relationships in particular.4Evidence also points to a link between economic stress and domestic violence.5Stock price movements represent shocks to wealth and may either exacerbate or relieve economic stress levels.Engelberg and Parsons (2016)find evidence of stock market having a meaningful impact on the stress levels of individuals, reflected in a significant negative relationship between local stock market returns and hospital admissions for psychological conditions.Schwandt (2018)shows that wealth shocks caused by the stock market predict wealth changes and strongly affect health outcomes, including physical health, mental health, and even survival rates.

We hypothesize that stock returns can affect stress levels within families and intimate relationships. The increased stress level driven by stock market losses could trigger and escalate arguments, resulting in increased incidence of domestic violence. To illustrate our hypothesis, we include a simple loss-of-control model adapted from that ofCard and Dahl (2011)inInternet Appendix Section IA.1. In addition to predicting the negative link between stock market returns and the incidence of domestic violence, the illustrative model highlights the importance of reference points in the form of expected stock returns. The model also predicts that stock market losses should have a stronger effect than stock market gains.

To test these predictions, we construct a large sample of domestic violence incidents in the United States, using data from the National Incident Based Reporting System (NIBRS) that include all reports of crime by city and county for the participating police agencies. The agencies in our data cover a population of 39 million in 2001, the first year of our sample. The coverage grows to 86 million in 2016, as more agencies gradually join the system. We calculate daily and weekly incident rates, defined as the number of reported incidents per 100,000 capita, at the level of individual police agencies. In our analysis, we define domestic violence as reported incidents of assault, aggravated assault, or intimidation by a spouse, partner, or boyfriend/girlfriend.6

We then construct a local stock market index for each U.S. state, calculated as the market-cap-weighted average return of all stocks headquartered in the state. Similar toEngelberg and Parsons (2016), our methodology utilizes the well-documented tendency of investors to overweight local stocks in their portfolios. Hence, our state-level stock index is likely to be a good proxy for the returns to investors in the same state.7This setup allows us to exploit the cross-sectional variation between states for each time period, in addition to the variation across time within the state, which increases the robustness of our analysis.8While not all families participate in the stock market, more than 20|$\%$|of U.S. households do have direct investments in stocks, and approximately 50|$\%$|have direct or indirect stock investments (Bricker et al. 2014;Bricker and Li 2017). As discussed below, our estimates of stock market participation in our data are consistent with these figures. These ratios clearly indicate that a sufficient share of the population is exposed to stock market movements to have a noticeable impact on their stress levels and consequent domestic violence incidents.

We perform our analysis at the weekly level. To study the relationship between weekly stock market returns and levels of domestic violence, for each week, we only include the incidents taking place during the weekend between Friday 4 p.m. (stock market closing) and Sunday 12 a.m. (midnight), while the weekly stock returns are calculated from Monday to Friday. We use weekly frequency because weekly stock market returns represent larger and more meaningful wealth shocks from market movements than daily returns, while still allowing us to capture the relatively instantaneous effect of return shocks for a causal interpretation. Moreover, as shown inFigure 1, the rates of domestic violence are higher during the weekend, when stock markets are closed, than during weekdays.

Based on our hypothesis, we anticipate that negative stock market returns should increase stress levels within families, trigger and escalate arguments, and result in higher levels of domestic violence. To illustrate this relationship, inFigure 2, we plot the seasonally adjusted average level of domestic violence and stock market returns around the week of October 6–12, 2008, the largest weekly stock market drop in our data. This figure suggests a visible negative association between domestic violence and stock returns. During the week of the main stock market crash, domestic violence increases pronouncedly, while the following week experiences both strongly positive stock returns and significantly reduced levels of domestic violence.9

This week experienced the largest weekly stock market drop in our data. Open in new tab Download slide

Our regression results provide more general support for the hypothesis. We find a significant negative relationship between state-level stock market returns and rates of domestic violence. This relationship is robust to controlling for police agency-month joint fixed effects, holiday fixed effects, and week-of-the-year fixed effects capturing conceivable seasonality within the year. We also control for state-level weekly insured unemployment rate, which is the only relevant macroeconomic variable available at weekly intervals. Utilizing the cross-sectional dimension of the data, we also show that the result holds when including week fixed effects, thus capturing only the cross-sectional variation between agencies for each week. This result provides further comfort in the identification, in addition to the time-series correlation that, for example, the analysis ofEngelberg and Parsons (2016)relies on.10Furthermore, in theInternet Appendix, we show that the significant negative relationship between local stock returns and domestic violence only holds for the concurrent week, while lagged or forward stock returns have no significant predictive power over domestic violence. This finding further supports a causal interpretation of the negative correlation.

We also find that the relationship between stock returns and domestic violence is larger in magnitude and statistically more significant for negative stock returns. Consistently, exceptionally large negative returns are associated with significantly higher levels of domestic violence. For example, the effect of at least a 13|$\%$|drop in weekly return on domestic violence is nearly four times as big as that of at least a 7|$\%$|drop in weekly return.11

Consistent withCard and Dahl (2011), our results suggest that unexpected losses are associated with stronger emotional cues and hence a larger effect on the incidence of domestic violence. The results ofDa, Huang, and Jin (2021)suggest that investors’ return expectations are significantly affected by recent past returns, with the most recent 4 weeks being the most influential for extrapolative beliefs. Hence, we perform the same analysis using returns relative to the last 4 weeks’ average return and find that the magnitude of the effect is more stable across different model specifications. We also find that the directionality of the relationship is much clearer when controlling for reference points. When using returns relative to the 4-week average, only losses appear to have a significant negative relationship with domestic violence. Furthermore, the effect of such losses increases in a monotone fashion with the magnitude of the loss.

A clear interpretation of the relationship between stock returns and domestic violence requires that an adequate number of households are exposed to the stock market. We thus construct a location-based measure of stock market participation using the Individual Income Tax Return Statistics from the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) and divide the agencies in our sample into three categories each year, based on the level of stock market participation at the agency location. We find that the effect of stock returns on the incidence of domestic violence is strongest in areas with the highest stock market participation, consistent with our hypothesis.12,13

A potential concern is that our findings might be driven by local firm events, such as layoffs or plant closures, which also may be correlated with negative local stock returns. This would mean that the channel is not stock-market-related financial stress but the associated economic stress. To address this issue, we use three alternative proxies for stock market that are unlikely to be significantly correlated with local economic events. First, we construct a state-level stock index for each police agency, excluding companies based in any county which the police agency covers. We then perform our baseline regression analysis using these indexes. The results are consistent, suggesting that our findings are not driven by very local economic factors. Second, we show that the results are robust to using the S&P 500 index returns instead of state-level stock returns, further alleviating the concern that local firm events might affect the findings. Finally, we construct a national stock index excluding the firms headquartered in the same state where the agency is located. The results remain qualitatively similar when using this index.14In addition, the findings are robust to using a nonlogarithmic form of the domestic violence rate or a dummy indicating a nonzero number of incidents as the dependent variable for an extensive margin analysis.

To verify that our findings are specific to domestic violence and not a reflection of other general patterns in crime rates, we construct similar incidence rates for other offenses that might conceivably be correlated with stock returns. These include assaults where the victim is unknown to the perpetrator, murders, sex offenses, robberies, and drug offenses. We show that none of these other types of crime exhibits a similar significant relationship. This finding further alleviates the concern that our results are driven by local economic conditions, as no other crimes are significantly affected by the local stock market movements.

Our estimates also suggest that the economic magnitude of the effect is not trivial. We find that a 13|$\%$|decrease in weekly stock return relative to 4-week average leads to an estimated 2.7|$\%$|increase in domestic violence around the average rate.15The coefficient we estimate for a dummy variable that indicates a local stock market drop of at least 13|$\%$|relative to 4-week average leads to an increase in domestic violence of 4.6|$\%$|from the average rate.16For comparison,Card and Dahl (2011)estimate that “upset losses” in professional football by the home team lead to a roughly 10|$\%$|increase in at-home male-on-female intimate partner violence on Sundays during the season.Engelberg and Parsons (2016)report an increase of more than 5|$\%$|in hospital admissions associated with the Black Monday stock market crash of almost 25|$\%$|⁠.

We perform a number of robustness checks to validate our results. These checks include different specifications of the domestic violence (LHS) variable, different definitions of weekend and different stock return periods, using Google search volumes for “domestic violence” as an alternative proxy for the incidence of domestic violence, different stock return reference points, analyses excluding states with the most listed companies, and analyses controlling for firm news, Google search volumes for “unemployment,” and stock return volatility. Our findings on the negative relationship between stock returns and domestic violence remain robust to all these alternative specifications.

Our study provides the first evidence of a link between stock market returns and domestic violence. Domestic violence triggered by stock returns could be viewed as a negative externality of stock market movements that affects household utility beyond the shock to financial wealth. Such externalities might, in part, help explain some of the implied high risk aversion or the limited stock market participation of households.17Furthermore, the asymmetric shape of the relationship between stock returns and domestic violence suggests that the emotional disutility of stock market losses differs from the utility of gains. Our finding that losses are associated with a substantially stronger effect on domestic violence than gains is consistent with several gain-loss models attempting to explain the equity market premium (Benartzi and Thaler 1995) or the disposition effect (e.g.,Odean 1998;Barberis and Xiong 2009;Barberis 2013;Ingersoll and Jin 2013). We contribute to the literature by providing real-world evidence that complements the experimental support for the asymmetric utility of gains and losses (e.g.,Thaler and Johnson 1990;Weber and Camerer 1998;Frydman et al. 2014;Kuhnen 2015;Imas 2016).

Our study also provides evidence of the social aspects of the disutility caused by stock market losses. An incident of domestic violence triggered by stock market losses extends the scope for disutility beyond the pure financial wealth shock as it affects the social relationships of the stockholder. This finding is related to the literature on the social implications of stock market losses. For example,Heimer (2016)shows that social interactions can be an important driver of the disutility of stock market losses, contributing to the disposition effect.

We also contribute by adding novel evidence of the economic causes of domestic violence. While most of the related studies focus on intra-household bargaining and outside options (e.g.,Tauchen, Witte, and Long 1991;Aizer 2010;Anderberg et al. 2016), we show that negative wealth shocks can generate emotional cues that may lead to arguments escalating and manifesting in domestic violence. This finding is consistent with and complements that ofCard and Dahl (2011).
1.1 Emotional utility of gains and losses

Financial losses may entail negative emotional shocks, while gains can cause positive ones. Meanwhile, the prospect theory ofKahneman and Tversky (1979)suggests that the disutility from negative wealth shocks is different from the utility from equivalent gains. This asymmetric utility from stock returns is used as the foundation for a number of models seeking to explain empirical patterns in the financial markets, including the equity premium puzzle (Benartzi and Thaler 1995) and the disposition effect (e.g.,Shefrin and Statman 1985;Odean 1998;Barberis 2013).

While the real-world evidence is limited, a number of experimental studies suggest that people react differently to losses compared to gains. These includeThaler and Johnson (1990),Weber and Camerer (1998),Kuhnen (2015),Frydman et al. (2014), andImas (2016).Meyer and Pagel (2022)show that individuals are more likely to reinvest realized capital gains than losses, using mutual fund liquidations as exogenous sale events.Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012)find that the probability of selling shares is V shaped around zero, suggesting that the effect of gains and losses is more complex than a simple symmetric relationship of gains versus losses.

An important application of the gain-loss utility are models attempting to explain the disposition effect ofShefrin and Statman (1985), the tendency of investors to realize winning rather than losing investments.Heath, Huddart, and Lang (1999)show that past stock returns and reference prices matter for stock option exercise. A large number of papers attempt to use prospect theory to explain the disposition effect.Barberis and Xiong (2009)consider both annual and realized gains and losses and conclude that the realized gains-losses model predicts a disposition effect more reliably.Ingersoll and Jin (2013)develop a model of realization utility with reference-dependent preferences that predicts a number of anomalies, including the disposition effect.

More recent studies also explore the determinants of the disposition effect. For example,Heimer and Imas (2022)find that financial constraints can reduce the disposition effect, whileAn et al. (2021)find that portfolio-level unrealized gains or losses affect the disposition effect for a stock.Heimer (2016)shows that social interactions can be an important driver of the disutility of stock market losses, contributing to the disposition effect.

1.2 Domestic violence in the economic literature

Economic theories incorporating domestic violence can be broadly divided into two categories. The first includes economic models of household bargaining where domestic violence arises because it provides positive utility to the perpetrator.18The victims’ willingness to endure violence is determined by their outside options. Early noncooperative models of the family including domestic violence as a source of gratification and instrument of control includeTauchen, Witte, and Long (1991)andFarmer and Tiefenthaler (1997). In related work,Pollak (2004)develops a model in which children adopt behavioral patterns with respect to domestic violence from their parents.

In the empirical literature following this framework,Aizer (2010)shows that decreases in the male-female wage gap reduce violence against women.Anderberg et al. (2016)show that an increase in male unemployment decreases the incidence of domestic violence, while an increase in female unemployment does the opposite.Bloch and Rao (2002)find evidence of men using domestic violence as a bargaining tool to extract transfers from the wife’s family in the context of rural India.

The second strand of literature suggests that domestic violence can be triggered unintentionally when an argument escalates out of control. This channel is highlighted byGelles and Straus (1989)andKelly and Johnson (2008), among others.Card and Dahl (2011)develop a more formal loss-of-control model to study the link between family violence and the emotional cues associated with unexpected wins and losses by professional football home teams. In their empirical analysis, they find that upset losses lead to significant increases in at-home violence by men against their wives and girlfriends. Similarly,Beland and Brent (2018)show that extreme traffic congestion is associated with significant increases in domestic violence.

A substantial body of literature links domestic violence to economic hardship.Conger et al. (1990)study the negative impact of economic hardship on marital quality.Gelles and Straus (1989)describe the “typical” abuser as someone worrying about economic security and dissatisfied with his standard of living.Benson et al. (2003)find evidence of financial strain and employment instability being related to domestic violence.

Another notable strand of literature focuses on the link between domestic violence and substance abuse.de Bruijn and de Graaf (2016)review the literature on the role of substance abuse in domestic violence and conclude that there is robust evidence of alcohol use increasing the likelihood of perpetrating physical violence. Some evidence suggests that cocaine use is associated with increased domestic violence perpetration and victimization.Luca, Owens, and Sharma (2015)study the impact of alcohol prohibition in India and find evidence that restricting access to alcohol may help reduce domestic violence.Lin and Lin (2021)find that domestic violence increases after the legalization of recreational marijuana. Evidence indicates that treatment for substance abuse can help reduce domestic violence as well (e.g.,Murphy and Ting 2010). Finally,Moffitt et al. (2000)study to what extent perpetrators of domestic violence are the same as or similar to the perpetrators of other crimes. They find that domestic violence and general crime represent different constructs that are moderately related; they are not two expressions of the same underlying antisocial propensity.19

1.3 Domestic violence and stock market returns

We argue that large stock market movements may also have an immediate effect on the stress levels of individuals and hence trigger and escalate arguments, similar to the impact of unexpected football losses documented byCard and Dahl (2011). This argument is in line with the results ofEngelberg and Parsons (2016), who find a connection between local stock returns and hospital admissions for psychological conditions.Schwandt (2018)finds more direct evidence that wealth shocks caused by the stock market can have detrimental effects. His results suggest that such shocks predict wealth changes and have an adverse effect on a number of health outcomes, including physical health, mental health, and survival rates.Kalcheva, McLemore, and Sias (2021)find that economic policy uncertainty is associated with increases in unhealthy behaviors by lowering impulse control.

Lin, Chen, and Liu (2015)find a connection between mental disorders and stock market fluctuations in Taiwan. The medical literature provides further indirect support.Chen et al. (2012)find that daily falls in the stock market index are associated with a higher incidence of stroke in Taiwan. Similarly, two studies using data from China find that stock market volatility is associated with higher levels of deaths due to coronary heart disease (Ma et al. 2011) and higher cardiovascular mortality (Lin et al. 2013).

A necessary condition for stock returns to have an effect on the level of domestic violence is that a substantial number of people hold stocks. Reassuringly, the literature shows that more than 20|$\%$|of U.S. households have direct investments in stocks, while approximately 50|$\%$|have direct or indirect stock investments (Bricker et al. 2014;Bricker and Li 2017). In our domestic violence data, we do not observe what stocks perpetrators are holding. Hence, we followEngelberg and Parsons (2016)to exploit the tendency of investors to overweight local stocks (home bias) to identify the relevant stock returns that induce domestic violence.20

In theInternet Appendix IA.1, we follow the approach inCard and Dahl (2011)to construct a simple loss-of-control model for the incidence of domestic violence. This illustrative model suggests a negative relationship between the stock return and the likelihood of domestic violence. As inCard and Dahl (2011), the gains and losses are measured relative to expectations, such that the reference point also matters. For example,Da, Huang, and Jin (2021)andGreenwood and Shleifer (2014)show evidence that investors extrapolate from recent past stock returns, with more weight on more recent returns, and that such extrapolative beliefs are stronger among nonprofessionals. Their findings indicate that recent past returns represent a good proxy for individual investors’ expectations of future returns. Finally, as suggested by the prospect theory (Kahneman and Tversky 1979), negative shocks are likely to have a stronger effect than positive ones, implying an asymmetric utility from gains and losses.

Based on these observations, we formulate our main hypothesis as follows:

Hypothesis:The incidence of domestic violence is negatively related to the difference between the realized local stock market returns and the investors’ expectations, and the effect is larger for losses than for gains.
article.2. Data and Methodology:
2.1 Domestic violence data

We obtain data on reported incidents of intimate partner violence from the National Incident Based Reporting System (NIBRS), which includes all reports of crime filed by individual police agencies. The number of agencies increases over time, as more agencies join the NIBRS.21The NIBRS data include a large number of small agencies, which have many weekly observations with zero incidents reported. To improve the consistency of the agencies in our sample, and to reduce the noise introduced by a large number of zero observations, we include agencies only covering populations of at least 10,000. The NIBRS database is available from 1991 onward, but its coverage in the early years is very low, which could lead to poor representativeness of the data for those years. Hence, we cut our sample period from 2001 onward. This is the first year with more than 1,000 agencies covering a population of above 10,000.Table 1shows the number of agencies in our sample each year. The agencies included cover a population of 86.2 million in 2016, the last year in our data, compared with 39.0 million in 2001.

The incident reports include the date and the time (by the hour) of the incident, as well as a number of other details. It is important to note that the incidents do not necessarily result in arrests, so the coverage of the data is broader than arrested or prosecuted cases. Similar toCard and Dahl (2011), we define domestic violence as a reported incident of assault, aggravated assault, or intimidation by a spouse, partner, or boyfriend or girlfriend. For calculating incident rates, we include all incidents satisfying these criteria, which means that our definition is less restrictive than that adopted byCard and Dahl (2011), who focus on male-on-female domestic violence occurring at home only.

We construct domestic violence rates, defined as the number of reported incidents per 100,000 capita, at the level of individual police agencies. Our main analysis is performed on the basis of weekly observations. To establish a relationship between weekly stock market returns and levels of domestic violence, for each week, we include only the incidents taking place during the weekend, between Friday 4 p.m. (stock market closing) and Sunday 12 a.m. (midnight), while the weekly stock returns are calculated from Monday to Friday. Compared with daily returns, the weekly stock return is more likely to generate a meaningful wealth shock to households, while still allowing us to capture the instantaneous effect of return shocks for a causal interpretation. Moreover, as incidents of domestic violence happen much more often on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, than on other weekdays (as shown inFigure 1), we focus on the relationship between weekly stock returns and domestic violence during these 3 days.

2.2 Local stock market returns

We obtain stock market data from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) for all U.S. stocks listed on NYSE, NASDAQ, and AMEX. We combine the stock data with historical company location data from Compustat. As an additional source of location data, we download all 10-K reports available in electronic format in the EDGAR database and add locations missing in Compustat based on these reports. These combined data yield approximately 90|$\%$|of the stock-day observations in CRSP during our sample period of 2001–2016. We then construct weekly state-level stock market index returns as market-cap-weighted average returns of all listed companies headquartered in each state. We use these state-level indexes as a proxy for local stock returns for our analysis, similar toEngelberg and Parsons (2016)who use Californian stock returns.

3.2 Stock market returns and incidence of domestic violence

Our hypothesis predicts a negative relationship between stock market returns and domestic violence. To test this prediction, we use regressions of the following form: $$\begin{equation}
ln(1+{\it DV \ rate})_{i,s,t} = \beta {\it Return}_{s,t} + \gamma X_{i,s,t} + \epsilon_{i,t},
\end{equation}$$ (1) where |$ln(1+DV \ rate)_{i,s,t}$| is the natural logarithm of one plus the domestic violence rate ( DV rate ) of agency |$i$|⁠ , located in state |$s$|⁠ , during week |$t$|⁠ . |$Return_{s,t}$| is the return of the stock market index for state |$s$| during week |$t$|⁠ , and |$X_{i,s,t}$| is a vector of controls, including the weekly insured unemployment (IU) rate , and depending on the specification, agency fixed effects (2,139 agencies), agency-year joint fixed effects , agency-quarter joint fixed effects , or agency-month joint fixed effects to capture time-variant factors like local economic conditions at the state level, holiday fixed effects with dummies for major holidays taking place during the week |$t$| (19 different holidays), and week-of-year fixed effects (52 weeks) to capture any seasonal effects. 23 In all regressions, we cluster standard errors by agency.

The results, shown in panel A ofTable 3, are consistent with our hypothesis. In all model specifications, the incidence of domestic violence during weekends is significantly negatively related to stock market returns during the same week. As shown by Model 4, the result is robust to including agency-month joint fixed effects that capture all local economic factors at the monthly frequency. This model specification mitigates the potential concern that our results are driven by other economic factors that are correlated with stock returns. For example, during economic downturns, stock returns are likely to be lower, while the economic hardship could plausibly cause increased levels of domestic violence, independent of stock returns. In addition, we control for weekly insured unemployment rate, reported at the state level, which is the only macroeconomic variable available at weekly intervals. Finally, as shown by Model 5, the result also holds when including week fixed effects, capturing essentially only cross-sectional differences across agencies for any given week.

To explore the impact of reference points, we also include a specification using weekly return relative to the rolling 4-week average stock return: $$\begin{equation}
ln(1+DV \ rate)_{i,s,t} = \beta \Delta Return_{s,t} + \gamma X_{i,s,t} + \epsilon_{i,t},
\end{equation}$$ (2) where |$\Delta Return_{s,t}$| is the current week stock return less the average weekly return of the last 4 weeks. This specification thus uses the rolling 4-week average return as a time-variant reference point, against which the current week’s gains or losses are measured. 24

The results, shown in panel B ofTable 3, also show a consistently negative and statistically significant relationship between stock returns relative to recent past returns and domestic violence. The most striking difference between the results for raw stock returns, reported in panel A, and reference-dependent stock returns in panel B, is the consistency of the magnitude of the estimated effect. In panel A, the estimated coefficients are substantially higher for the models with a smaller number of fixed effects and decrease when including agency-time fixed effects at shorter intervals. In contrast, the estimated coefficients for reference-dependent stock returns are remarkably stable across all model specifications. This result suggests that the regression specification in panel B appears to fit our illustrative model inSection IA.1better. In other words, reference points in the form of expected stock returns matter for the impact of current stock returns on the incidence of domestic violence.

3.3 Gains versus losses and domestic violence

Consistent with the prospect theory ofKahneman and Tversky (1979), we hypothesize that stock market losses have a stronger effect than gains. To test this prediction, we perform an analysis with returns separated into positive and negative ones. The results, shown inTable 4, indicate that the effect of stock returns is stronger for negative stock returns. For raw stock market returns, the estimated coefficient for positive returns is also negative, suggesting that higher returns are associated with lower levels of domestic violence. However, this relationship for positive returns is weaker in both magnitude and statistical significance. Interestingly, when using returns relative to the 4-week average, the directionality of the result becomes significantly stronger. The estimated coefficient for positive returns is statistically insignificant, while the coefficient for negative returns is large and highly statistically significant. At the bottom of the table, we also reportp-values from an F-test of the difference between the positive and negative coefficients. For the specifications including a reference point (Models 3 and 4), the coefficients are significantly different, withp-values below 1|$\%$|⁠. These findings are consistent with our hypothesis and suggest that the emotional disutility of stock market losses is stronger than the utility from gains.

Next, we perform a separate analysis of the relationship between exceptional losses and domestic violence by including dummies for weeks where the state-level stock market exhibits negative returns of at least a given magnitude. For example, the variableDrop 7|$\%$|takes the value one if the weekly stock market return is negative 7|$\%$|or lower. We include several different magnitudes of negative returns. We also perform this analysis using drops relative to the 4-week average to investigate the impact of having a reference point.Table 5reports the results. For raw stock market returns, the estimated coefficients for the drop dummies increase in an almost monotone fashion with the magnitude of loss. This means that the larger the loss, the larger an increase in domestic violence it is associated with. For instance, the effect of a 13|$\%$|drop in weekly return on domestic violence is nearly four times as big as that of a 7|$\%$|drop. This relationship becomes even clearer when using returns relative to the 4-week average. The estimated coefficients for stock market drops relative to past returns increase in a perfectly monotone fashion, and all of the estimates are statistically significant. These results suggest that the main driver of the negative relationship between stock returns and domestic violence is extreme negative return events. In theInternet Appendix(Table IA.11), we also include a model specification controlling for stock volatility, which yields similar results.

article.4. Additional Analysis:
4.1 Stock returns and stock market participation

As we discussed in Section1.3, a causal interpretation of the relationship between stock returns and domestic violence is only plausible if an adequate number of households is exposed to the stock market. To study the role of stock market participation, we construct a location-based measure of stock market participation using the Individual Income Tax Return (Form 1040) Statistics from the IRS. These data include ZIP-code-level data of types of income and households. We define stock market participation as the proportion of households in a given ZIP code that reports dividend income during the year. We then calculate population-weighted average level of stock market participation for each county and match those to agency locations. The tax data are available from 2004 onward, so the sample period for this analysis is 2004-2016.

Figures 4and5show the distribution of stock market participation in our sample both unconditionally and plotted against the income level. From these figures, we see (a) a clearly adequate amount of stock market participation in our sample generally and (b) substantial variation in stock market participation between different locations, even at similar income levels. Hence, we can exploit the variation in stock market participation to provide further evidence that the negative relationship between stock returns and the incidence of domestic violence is due to the wealth shocks caused by the stock market and not due to other unobserved events.

The estimated coefficients are negative for all stock market participation levels, but the lowest stock market participation category has the economically smallest and statistically insignificant coefficient. In the most robust specification, including agency-month fixed effects, the magnitude of the estimated coefficients increases monotonically with stock market participation, and the estimates are statistically significant only for the highest stock market participation category. This finding might even be underestimated given that the reported incidence of domestic violence is generally substantially lower in areas with high stock market participation, as illustrated inFigure 6.

4.2 Confounding local economic factors and variable specification

Our results could be driven by unobserved factors that simultaneously affect local communities and local stock returns. The most obvious candidates would be layoffs or facility closures by local firms, which might potentially result in increased levels of domestic violence and negative stock price reactions. The channel would not be stock-market-related financial stress but other economic stress. To address this concern, we first note that the results in the literature on layoff announcement returns are mixed. For example,Blackwell, Marr, and Spivey (1990)find a negative stock-market reaction to plant-closing announcements, whilePalmon, Sun, and Tang (1997)find that the direction of announcement returns depends on the reason cited for the layoffs. Furthermore, the magnitude of announcement returns is relatively modest.Chalos and Chen (2003)find a positive market reaction to layoff announcements related to revenue refocusing, insignificant reaction to layoffs involving cost cutting, and weak evidence of a negative market reaction to layoffs related to plant closings.

To address the issue empirically, we perform an analysis with alternative proxies for stock market returns that are not likely to be affected by such local economic events. First, we construct a state-level stock index for each police agency, excluding the firms headquartered in any county which the police agency covers. We then perform our baseline regression analysis using these indexes as the independent variable. Second, we perform the same analysis using the S&P 500 index as the proxy for stock returns. These alternative proxies for stock returns are unlikely to be significantly affected by local economic events. Third, for each agency, we construct a national value-weighted stock market index excluding the firms headquartered in the same state where the agency is located.

Panel A ofTable 7indicates that the estimations using these alternative stock returns are consistent with the baseline result shown in column 1 for comparison. These results suggest that local economic factors do not drive our findings; thus, they remain consistent with our hypothesis that negative stock returns can trigger incidents of domestic violence via increased stress levels.

4.3 Other offense rates

As discussed above, we find a significant negative relationship between weekly stock returns and domestic violence during the weekend. To verify that our findings are specific to domestic violence and not a reflection of other general patterns in crime rates, we construct similar incidence rates for other offenses that might conceivably be correlated with stock returns. We include analyses on (a)assaults, defined as aggravated assault, assault, or intimidation, where the victim is unknown to the perpetrator, (b)murders, (c)sex offenses, including rape, sodomy, sexual assault with an object, and fondling, (d)robberies, and (d)drug offenses, including drug/narcotic violations and drug equipment violations.

The results, shown inTable 8, provide no evidence of a similar significant relationship between weekly stock returns and offense rates during the weekend for any of the other types of offense. These results support our hypothesis that stock returns are relevant due to the stress they induce in relationships and hence trigger incidents of domestic violence. Such changes in stress levels do not appear to translate into differences in the other offense rates we analyze.

4.4 Other additional analyses

We perform a number of additional analyses with results reported in theInternet Appendix. We summarize the findings in this section.

(i)Gender and gender inequality.InSection IA.2.1, we explore the role of gender and gender inequality in the relationship between stock returns and domestic violence. We find that the negative relationship between stock market returns and domestic violence holds for both genders, although the estimated coefficient is both economically larger and statistically more significant for male perpetrators. We note that (a) this finding might be affected by the fact that less than 20|$\%$|of the incidents in our data are committed by women and (b) the estimated relative change in domestic violence rates conditional on stock returns is very similar for both genders. Furthermore, we find that the negative relationship between stock market returns and domestic violence perpetrated by women exists only in states with relatively low gender inequality. In contrast, the relationship between domestic violence perpetrated by men and stock returns is stronger in states with higher gender inequality.(ii)Incidents with and without alcohol involved.InSection IA.2.2, we study the role of alcohol in our results. We divide the incidents of domestic violence in our sample into those reported to have involved alcohol, and those not. We find that non-alcohol-related domestic violence appears substantially more negatively associated with stock returns. This finding might suggest that either the types of incidents driven by stock market losses or the types of households involved are different from alcohol-related incidents of domestic violence.(iii)Retail-oriented stocks.The premise of our study is that wealth shocks caused by the stock market can affect the emotional stress levels within households and, in the extreme cases, escalate arguments into domestic violence. As we discussed in Section4.1, this premise requires an adequate number of households to hold stocks. In that section, we showed that our results are indeed strongest in areas with the highest stock market participation levels. InSection IA.2.3, we construct various indexes overweighting stocks more likely to be held by households. We find that the relationship between domestic violence and stock market returns is robust to using any of these alternative retail-oriented indexes.(iv)Stock returns and regional income level.The rate of stock market participation is positively correlated with income level, as shown by, for example,Chien and Morris (2017). Conversely, domestic violence is more prevalent among households at lower income levels. Given these opposite correlations of domestic violence and stock market participation with income level, inSection IA2.4, we study from which income groups the relationship between stock returns and reported domestic violence comes from. We divide the agencies in our sample into three categories each year, based on the average personal income per capita at the agency location, and then include dummies indicating these wealth categories in our regressions. The estimated relationship between stock returns and domestic violence is negative for all income levels, but the middle tertile exhibits the strongest and statistically most significant relationship between stock returns and domestic violence. The relationship is not statistically significant in the top tertile, and the estimated coefficient is also substantially smaller in magnitude. This result is intuitive, as households in the middle part of the income distribution are likely to have a large enough exposure to the stock market and also represent a high enough proportion of the reported rates of domestic violence. It also should be noted that domestic violence exists even in the highest income categories, and a meaningful proportion of households have stock investments in the lowest income categories.

4.5 Robustness checks

We perform a battery of robustness check analyses that are reported in theInternet Appendix. The results will be briefly discussed in this section.

(i)Google search volume as an alternative indicator of domestic violence.A potential concern related to data on domestic violence based on incidents reported to police agencies is that not all incidents get reported. This well-known underreporting issue leads to understating true incidence levels of domestic violence and possibly results in a bias in our results if there are systematic differences in the propensity to report. To mitigate this concern, inSection IA.3.1, we use state-level Google search volume for “domestic violence” as an alternative proxy for the incidence of domestic violence. It seems plausible that victims (or perpetrators) of domestic violence might conduct online searches involving this phrase following incidents of domestic violence. Compared with reporting to police, there is less scope for reporting biases that could drive local stock returns and Google search volume. Similar to our main results, here we find a significant negative relationship between the weekly stock return and Google search volume for “domestic violence” during the weekend. The economic magnitude of the estimated effect appears larger than that based on reported domestic violence.(ii)Including lagged and forward returns.InSection IA3.2, we perform a regression analysis including lagged and forward local stock returns. We find that only concurrent week returns are statistically significantly related to levels of domestic violence, while the coefficients of both lagged and forward stock returns are statistically insignificant. This result supports a causal interpretation of the same-week effect, as it addresses the reverse causality concern. Moreover, the relevant economic factors that are related to stock returns are unlikely to change so rapidly such that they are not captured by the surrounding week’s stock returns. This result holds for both raw stock market returns and returns relative to the 4-week average.(iii)Period definitions for LHS and RHS variables.To make sure that our results are not driven by the choice of weekend period or weekly stock returns, inSection IA.3.3, we perform a regression analysis using different weekend periods for theDV rate, as well as different period lengths for stock returns. We find that our results are robust to various definitions of weekend, whether or not we include Monday morning until 9 a.m. (stock market opening) and whether we begin the weekend on Thursday or Friday. Across all of these various specifications, we find a statistically significant negative relationship between the weekly stock return and the incidence of domestic violence during the weekend. Similarly, when using the stock returns for any period between one and five most recent trading days before the weekend, this negative relationship remains statistically significant. Taken together, these results suggest that our findings are not driven by the choice of variable definitions.(iv)Different reference points.In our main analysis, we use the last 4-week average stock return as a reference point and estimate the effect of current stock return relative to this reference point. To make sure that our results are not driven by the choice of reference period, inSection IA.3.4, we replicate our main regression analysis using different period lengths for the reference stock return, ranging from the last 2 weeks to the last six months. We also include the weekly compound year-to-date (YTD) return as well as an exponential decay model, with declining weights for further past returns based on the decay estimates fromDa, Huang, and Jin (2021). The estimated coefficients remain statistically significant across all specifications. The magnitude is smaller for the 2- and 3-week reference points but appears more stable for 4 weeks and longer periods.(v)Excluding particular years, months, or states.InSection IA.3.5, we establish the robustness of our results to the exclusion of any single year, calendar month, or state from the sample. These results show that the relationship between stock market returns and domestic violence is not solely driven by a subset of influential observations. InSection IA.3.6, we also perform an analysis excluding states with the largest number of listed companies in our data, namely, Illinois, Massachusetts, and Texas. The states with generally the largest number of listed companies in the United States, that is, New York and California, are not included in our analysis. The results remain similar when excluding any or all of the states with the largest number of listed companies.(vi)Controlling for other determinants of domestic violence.To further make sure that our results are robust to controlling for general economic conditions, as well as to facilitate comparisons of our data with earlier studies of domestic violence, inSection IA.3.7, we include an additional regression analysis controlling for other determinants of domestic violence. These macroeconomic controls include the county-level personal income per capita, economic policy uncertainty (EPU) (Baker, Bloom, and Davis 2016), the investor sentiment index ofBaker and Wurgler (2006), male and female unemployment rates, female/male wage gap, population, and state-level Gender Equality Index calculated byDi Noia (2002). The relationship between stock market returns and domestic violence is robust to including all of these controls. These results also show that our data are consistent with the findings ofAnderberg et al. (2016)andAizer (2010).(vii)Controlling for stock market volatility.To explore whether the volatility of stock returns matters as well, inSection IA.3.8, we conduct an analysis controlling for the volatility of daily stock returns during the week. The estimated coefficients for volatility are not statistically significant in any of the model specifications, including or excluding the weekly stock return, while the estimated coefficients for stock returns remain negative and statistically significant across all specifications. Hence, volatility during the week does not appear to have a significant effect on the incidence of domestic violence.(viii)Controlling for firm news.To provide further comfort that the results are driven by the wealth shocks instead of other channels, inSection IA.3.2, we perform a regression analysis controlling for firm-related news. Specifically, we construct a firm-level news sentiment index using RavenPack News Analytics data. The negative relationship between stock returns and the incidence of domestic violence remains statistically significant and virtually unchanged compared to our main results.(ix)Controlling for Google search volume for unemployment.To further check that our findings relate to wealth shocks caused by the stock market, instead of other economic hardship like company layoffs, inSection IA.3.10, we perform an analysis controlling for weekly Google search volume for “unemployment.” This variable is likely to capture weekly changes in worries related to employment. The Google search volume index is at the state level. Our results are robust to controlling for this variable as well.(x)Aggregating data to state and national levels.Our agency-week data include a large number of observations with no incidents of domestic violence. To make sure that our findings are not affected by this sparsity in the domestic violence incidents, inSection IA.3.12, we perform a regression analysis using aggregated state- and national-level data, with very few zero observations. Our findings remain qualitatively similar to the agency-level results.(xi)Population-weighted regressions.As some of the agencies in our data cover smaller populations than others, one possible concern is that smaller places end up having a larger variation in domestic violence rates and disproportionately drive our results. To mitigate this concern, inSection IA.3.12, we perform weighted least squares (WLS) regression analyses both at the agency level (main sample) and aggregated to the state level, using the covered population (or its natural logarithm) as the weighting variable. The results remain qualitatively similar to our main results, suggesting that our findings are not substantially driven by agencies covering very small populations.(xii)Return index based on 10-K state name counts.In our main analysis, we use a state-level stock market index based on the headquarters locations of each firm. To make sure that our findings are not sensitive to this methodology of allocating firms to states, inSection IA3.13, we construct an alternative return index, using a different methodology to assign firms to different states. We use the data ofGarcía and Norli (2012)on the state name counts in firms’ 10-K filings to constructReturn - mentioned states, a state-level stock market return index based on the states mentioned in the firm’s 10-K filings. In this index, the firm’s market capitalization is allocated to different states in proportion to the number of mentions of each state, and these proportional market caps are used to weight stock returns. Our results remain similar using this alternative return index.
article.5. Conclusion:
Stock market movements, especially crashes, represent both financial and emotional shocks that can affect stress levels within families and trigger arguments. This disutility of emotional shocks represents a very specific externality of stock investments. We find that large stock market losses during the week are associated with noticeable increases in the incidence of domestic violence during the weekend. The relationship between stock market gains and domestic violence is much weaker or nonexistent.

Our findings have potentially important implications on understanding households’ investment decisions. First, they suggest that stock investments may entail risks that are broader than the effect of financial losses. If externalities of this type abound, they could help explain the seemingly high risk aversion implied by the realized stock market returns and return volatility, often referred to as the equity premium puzzle. If the volatility of the stock market causes variation in utility that is larger than that caused by the purely financial component of utility, then standard economic models measuring utility only by wealth might underestimate the total “risk” of stock investments.

Second, our results suggest that the impact of gains and losses on household utility is asymmetric, with losses outweighing gains of similar magnitude. This finding is consistent with several gain-loss models attempting to explain the equity market premium or anomalies, such as the disposition effect, and provides real-world evidence complementing a number of prior experimental studies.
